plan_of_care_meeting_feedback.q1_comments: I feel very clear on what the Plan of Care meeting is for and what we are trying to accomplish with it. The team explained that this is where we review how I’ve been doing over the last 90 days, look at my goals (like walking more steadily with my walker, managing my blood pressure and diabetes better, and staying as independent as possible at home), and decide if anything needs to change in my therapies or medications. Before the meeting, the social worker called me and went over the agenda in plain language, which helped a lot. During the meeting they showed me a printed copy of my care plan and walked me through each section—medical issues, physical therapy, occupational therapy, nursing visits, and even transportation to appointments. They also explained that I can ask for another meeting sooner if my condition changes or if I feel something isn’t working. I appreciated that they told me who is responsible for each part (for example, my primary nurse, my physical therapist, and my primary care doctor) so I know whom to call when I have questions. In short, I don’t feel like this is just a formality; I understand it as the main time when everyone gets on the same page about my care and makes sure the plan still fits my real life at home.
plan_of_care_meeting_feedback.q2_comments: The Plan of Care and the actual meeting have been very beneficial to my care because they forced us to look at how I’m really functioning day to day, not just what my numbers look like on paper. For example, when we reviewed my last 3 months, the nurse brought up that I had two near-falls in my kitchen when reaching for pots in the lower cabinets. That turned into a concrete change in the plan: the occupational therapist added specific home safety training and recommended a different storage setup and a reacher tool. Since then, I haven’t had any more near-falls in that area. Another example is my medication schedule. I admitted that I was sometimes skipping my midday dose of metformin if I napped through lunch. Because we talked about this openly at the meeting, the nurse and doctor adjusted the timing and my daughter and I set up a pill organizer with alarms on my phone. My blood sugar readings have been more stable the last several weeks. The meeting also helped coordinate between people who usually don’t talk directly—my physical therapist, my primary doctor, and the social worker. Instead of each one telling me something slightly different, they agreed together on realistic goals, like being able to walk safely from my bedroom to the bathroom at night without help, and climbing the 5 steps to my front door with just my cane. I left the meeting with a written list of goals and timelines, which makes it easier for me and my daughter to keep track. Overall, the PoC process has made me feel like there is a clear roadmap instead of just random appointments.
plan_of_care_meeting_feedback.q3_comments: I do feel included as a valued member of the decision-making team, especially compared to past experiences in other clinics where I felt like things were just decided for me. In this PoC meeting, they asked for my input several times before finalizing anything. For instance, when the physical therapist suggested increasing my therapy visits from once a week to twice a week, they didn’t just assume I could do it. They asked me about my energy levels, my transportation, and my other appointments. I was able to say that mornings are better for me because my knees are less stiff and my daughter can usually drive me then. They adjusted the plan so that I have morning sessions and they coordinated transportation for the one day my daughter works early. Another example is when we talked about my long-term goal of staying in my own home as long as possible. The social worker asked me very directly what I was most afraid of. I told her my biggest worry is falling in the shower when no one is around. That led to adding a shower chair and grab bars to the plan, and they scheduled a home safety visit. That made me feel listened to, not brushed off. The only reason I put “agree” instead of “strongly agree” is that sometimes the medical language still goes over my head at first and I have to ask them to repeat or rephrase. They are polite about it, but I still feel a little embarrassed asking. I think as I get more used to these meetings and learn the terms, I’ll feel even more like an equal partner. But overall, I definitely feel like my opinion matters and actually affects the plan.
plan_of_care_meeting_feedback.q4_comments: My questions and concerns were very thoroughly addressed during the PoC process. I came into the meeting with a handwritten list of things I wanted to talk about—like my increased nighttime leg cramps, my fear of falling when I’m alone, confusion about one of my blood pressure pills, and worries about being a burden on my daughter. The nurse practitioner went through the list point by point. For the leg cramps, she explained possible causes, checked my recent lab work, and suggested stretching exercises before bed, more consistent hydration, and a small change in one of my medications. She also ordered updated labs just to be safe. For my fear of falling, the physical therapist demonstrated specific balance exercises I can do while holding onto the kitchen counter and reviewed how to properly use my walker in tight spaces like the bathroom. They didn’t just say, “Be careful,” they gave me practical steps. Regarding my medication confusion, they took the time to redraw my medication chart in large print, with the pill colors and shapes written next to the names, and they removed one older medication that I no longer needed, which simplified my schedule. The social worker also addressed my emotional concern about burdening my daughter by talking about respite services and making sure I know there are other supports available if my daughter gets overwhelmed. Importantly, they didn’t rush me. The meeting went a little over the scheduled time, but nobody seemed annoyed. They even asked at the end if there were any questions I hadn’t had a chance to ask, which made me feel like it was truly okay to bring up anything on my mind.
plan_of_care_meeting_feedback.q5_comments: I am very comfortable bringing questions and concerns to any staff member during my PoC meetings and even in between meetings. Part of this is because they have consistently treated me with respect and patience, so I don’t feel like I’m bothering them. For example, at my last visit I forgot to ask about whether it was safe for me to try gentle yoga again. A few days later, I called the main number, and even though I reached the receptionist first, she took the question seriously, relayed it to the nurse, and I got a call back the same afternoon with a clear answer. That experience taught me that I don’t have to wait until the next formal meeting to speak up. During the PoC meeting itself, I felt comfortable interrupting politely if I didn’t understand something. At one point the doctor started talking about my A1C trend and used some technical terms. I said, “I’m sorry, can you say that again in simpler words?” and he immediately rephrased it without making me feel dumb. Also, they made a point of telling me that any staff member—nurses, social workers, therapists—would either answer my question or find the right person who could. This takes the pressure off of me to know exactly who is in charge of what. Knowing that my questions are welcome, and seeing that they actually follow up and adjust my care based on what I say, makes me feel very safe speaking my mind. I even feel comfortable bringing up sensitive topics, like feeling depressed on some days or being embarrassed about needing help with bathing, because they’ve always responded kindly and without judgment.
